questions.text: Which of the following best describes the difference between a program and a process as outlined in the text?

choices.label: A program is an active entity while a process is a passive entity stored on disk.
choices.text: A program is an active entity while a process is a passive entity stored on disk.

choices.label: A program is a passive entity, such as an executable file, while a process is an active entity.
choices.text: A program is a passive entity, such as an executable file, while a process is an active entity.

choices.label: A program and a process are entirely interchangeable terms in all operating systems.
choices.text: A program and a process are entirely interchangeable terms in all operating systems.

choices.label: A process exists only on disk, whereas a program exists only in memory.
choices.text: A process exists only on disk, whereas a program exists only in memory.

questions.explanation: According to the slides, a program is a passive entity (executable file on disk), whereas a process is an active entity that becomes such when the executable is loaded into memory.

questions.text: A process in memory is composed of multiple parts. Select all the sections that constitute a process's memory layout:

choices.label: Text section (code)
choices.text: Text section (code)

choices.label: Stack (temporary data like function parameters)
choices.text: Stack (temporary data like function parameters)

choices.label: Data section (global variables)
choices.text: Data section (global variables)

choices.label: Heap (dynamically allocated memory)
choices.text: Heap (dynamically allocated memory)

choices.label: Kernel Section (shared OS code)
choices.text: Kernel Section (shared OS code)

questions.explanation: A process in memory includes the text section, the stack (temporary data), the data section (global variables), and the heap (dynamically allocated memory during runtime).

questions.text: When a process is waiting to be assigned to a processor, it is in which of the following states?

choices.label: Waiting
choices.text: Waiting

choices.label: Running
choices.text: Running

choices.label: Ready
choices.text: Ready

choices.label: New
choices.text: New

questions.explanation: The 'Ready' state is defined as the process waiting to be assigned to a processor. 'Waiting' means it is waiting for an event (like I/O completion).

questions.text: Which component of the Process Control Block (PCB) contains the address of the next instruction to be executed for that process?

choices.label: Process state
choices.text: Process state

choices.label: CPU registers
choices.text: CPU registers

choices.label: Program counter
choices.text: Program counter

choices.label: I/O status information
choices.text: I/O status information

questions.explanation: The Program Counter indicates the address of the next instruction to be executed for the process.

questions.text: In the context of a CPU switch between processes, what is the primary purpose of the 'Context Switch'?

choices.label: To increase the degree of multiprogramming by adding more processes to the job queue.
choices.text: To increase the degree of multiprogramming by adding more processes to the job queue.

choices.label: To save the state of the current process and load the saved state of a new process.
choices.text: To save the state of the current process and load the saved state of a new process.

choices.label: To perform useful computation while the I/O devices are busy.
choices.text: To perform useful computation while the I/O devices are busy.

choices.label: To allocate more memory to a process so it can finish its execution faster.
choices.text: To allocate more memory to a process so it can finish its execution faster.

questions.explanation: A context switch is the process of saving the state of an old process and loading the saved state for a new process when the CPU switches.

questions.text: How does the introduction of threads change the traditional concept of a process in the PCB?

choices.label: It eliminates the need for a program counter.
choices.text: It eliminates the need for a program counter.

choices.label: It allows a process to have multiple program counters, enabling multiple locations to execute at once.
choices.text: It allows a process to have multiple program counters, enabling multiple locations to execute at once.

choices.label: It combines the stack and the heap into a single memory section.
choices.text: It combines the stack and the heap into a single memory section.

choices.label: It forces the process to remain in the 'Waiting' state indefinitely.
choices.text: It forces the process to remain in the 'Waiting' state indefinitely.

questions.explanation: Threads allow a process to have multiple program counters, which means multiple locations in the code can execute concurrently.

questions.text: In Linux, the process representation is handled by which C structure?

choices.label: `proc_struct`
choices.text: `proc_struct`

choices.label: `task_struct`
choices.text: `task_struct`

choices.label: `pcb_list`
choices.text: `pcb_list`

choices.label: `linux_process`
choices.text: `linux_process`

questions.explanation: In Linux, the process is represented by the C structure `task_struct`, which contains the PID, state, scheduling info, and more.

questions.text: The process scheduler maintains several queues for processes. Select all valid queues mentioned in the text:

choices.label: Job queue
choices.text: Job queue

choices.label: Ready queue
choices.text: Ready queue

choices.label: Device queues
choices.text: Device queues

choices.label: Terminated queue
choices.text: Terminated queue

questions.explanation: The text identifies the Job queue (all processes), Ready queue (main memory, ready to execute), and Device queues (waiting for I/O).

questions.text: A process that spends more of its time doing computations and has few, very long CPU bursts is known as a(n):

choices.label: I/O-bound process
choices.text: I/O-bound process

choices.label: Computation-bound process
choices.text: Computation-bound process

choices.label: CPU-bound process
choices.text: CPU-bound process

choices.label: Memory-bound process
choices.text: Memory-bound process

questions.explanation: A CPU-bound process is defined as one that spends more time doing computations and has few very long CPU bursts.

questions.text: The long-term scheduler is primarily responsible for which of the following?

choices.label: Selecting which process should be executed next by the CPU.
choices.text: Selecting which process should be executed next by the CPU.

choices.label: Controlling the degree of multiprogramming.
choices.text: Controlling the degree of multiprogramming.

choices.label: Saving the context of a process during a switch.
choices.text: Saving the context of a process during a switch.

choices.label: Managing the device queues for I/O requests.
choices.text: Managing the device queues for I/O requests.

questions.explanation: The long-term scheduler (job scheduler) selects which processes should be brought into the ready queue, thereby controlling the degree of multiprogramming.

questions.text: What is the primary function of a 'Medium-term scheduler'?

choices.label: To handle the context switch between two running processes.
choices.text: To handle the context switch between two running processes.

choices.label: To select the initial job from the disk to load into memory.
choices.text: To select the initial job from the disk to load into memory.

choices.label: To swap processes in and out of memory to reduce the degree of multiprogramming.
choices.text: To swap processes in and out of memory to reduce the degree of multiprogramming.

choices.label: To manage the communication between the client and the server.
choices.text: To manage the communication between the client and the server.

questions.explanation: The medium-term scheduler can remove a process from memory (swapping) to decrease the degree of multiprogramming and later bring it back to continue execution.

questions.text: On mobile systems like iOS, how does multitasking typically differ from traditional desktop multitasking?

choices.label: It allows an unlimited number of foreground processes.
choices.text: It allows an unlimited number of foreground processes.

choices.label: It typically allows only one foreground process while others are suspended or restricted in the background.
choices.text: It typically allows only one foreground process while others are suspended or restricted in the background.

choices.label: Background processes have no limits on CPU or memory usage.
choices.text: Background processes have no limits on CPU or memory usage.

choices.label: It does not use a process scheduler at all.
choices.text: It does not use a process scheduler at all.

questions.explanation: Early versions of iOS and similar mobile systems often restrict the user to a single foreground process, while background processes are either suspended or given very specific, limited capabilities (like audio playback).

questions.text: When a parent process creates a child process in UNIX using the `fork()` system call, what is the initial state of the child's address space?

choices.label: It is empty until an `exec()` call is made.
choices.text: It is empty until an `exec()` call is made.

choices.label: It is a duplicate of the parent's address space.
choices.text: It is a duplicate of the parent's address space.

choices.label: It is assigned a random segment of the heap.
choices.text: It is assigned a random segment of the heap.

choices.label: It shares the exact same physical memory pages as the parent without duplication.
choices.text: It shares the exact same physical memory pages as the parent without duplication.

questions.explanation: In UNIX, the `fork()` system call creates a new process that is a duplicate of the parent's address space.

questions.text: A process that has finished execution but whose parent has not yet called `wait()` is known as a(n):

choices.label: Orphan process
choices.text: Orphan process

choices.label: Zombie process
choices.text: Zombie process

choices.label: Daemon process
choices.text: Daemon process

choices.label: Cascading process
choices.text: Cascading process

questions.explanation: A process becomes a 'zombie' if it has terminated but its parent hasn't called `wait()` to collect its status.

questions.text: If a parent process terminates before its children, and the operating system does not allow children to continue, what occurs?

choices.label: The children become zombies.
choices.text: The children become zombies.

choices.label: The children are adopted by the `init` process.
choices.text: The children are adopted by the `init` process.

choices.label: Cascading termination occurs.
choices.text: Cascading termination occurs.

choices.label: The children are granted root privileges.
choices.text: The children are granted root privileges.

questions.explanation: Cascading termination is when the operating system initiates the termination of all children, grandchildren, etc., because the parent process has terminated.

questions.text: Google Chrome uses a multiprocess architecture. Select the three types of processes it employs:

choices.label: Browser process
choices.text: Browser process

choices.label: Renderer process
choices.text: Renderer process

choices.label: Plug-in process
choices.text: Plug-in process

choices.label: Execution process
choices.text: Execution process

choices.label: Kernel process
choices.text: Kernel process

questions.explanation: Google Chrome utilizes Browser processes (UI/Disk/Network), Renderer processes (Web pages/HTML/JS), and Plug-in processes.

questions.text: In the Producer-Consumer problem, what is the key difference between an 'unbounded-buffer' and a 'bounded-buffer'?

choices.label: Unbounded-buffer uses message passing, while bounded-buffer uses shared memory.
choices.text: Unbounded-buffer uses message passing, while bounded-buffer uses shared memory.

choices.label: Unbounded-buffer has no practical limit on the size of the buffer, whereas bounded-buffer has a fixed size.
choices.text: Unbounded-buffer has no practical limit on the size of the buffer, whereas bounded-buffer has a fixed size.

choices.label: Unbounded-buffer is only for CPU-bound processes.
choices.text: Unbounded-buffer is only for CPU-bound processes.

choices.label: Bounded-buffer allows the consumer to produce items.
choices.text: Bounded-buffer allows the consumer to produce items.

questions.explanation: Unbounded-buffer places no practical limit on size; bounded-buffer assumes a fixed buffer size.

questions.text: What are the two primary models of Interprocess Communication (IPC)?

choices.label: Direct and Indirect
choices.text: Direct and Indirect

choices.label: Shared Memory and Message Passing
choices.text: Shared Memory and Message Passing

choices.label: Sockets and RPCs
choices.text: Sockets and RPCs

choices.label: Blocking and Non-blocking
choices.text: Blocking and Non-blocking

questions.explanation: The two models of IPC described are Shared Memory and Message Passing.

questions.text: In a message-passing system, if communication is 'Indirect', where are messages sent to?

choices.label: Directly to the receiver's process ID.
choices.text: Directly to the receiver's process ID.

choices.label: To a shared variable in the data section.
choices.text: To a shared variable in the data section.

choices.label: To mailboxes or ports.
choices.text: To mailboxes or ports.

choices.label: To the short-term scheduler.
choices.text: To the short-term scheduler.

questions.explanation: In indirect communication, messages are directed to and received from mailboxes, also referred to as ports.

questions.text: If both the `send()` and `receive()` operations are 'Blocking', it results in which of the following scenarios?

choices.label: A race condition
choices.text: A race condition

choices.label: A rendezvous
choices.text: A rendezvous

choices.label: An orphan process
choices.text: An orphan process

choices.label: Automatic buffering
choices.text: Automatic buffering

questions.explanation: When both send and receive are blocking, it is considered a rendezvous between the sender and the receiver.

questions.text: What does a 'Zero capacity' buffer in a message-passing system imply for the sender?

choices.label: The sender never waits.
choices.text: The sender never waits.

choices.label: The sender must wait for the receiver (rendezvous).
choices.text: The sender must wait for the receiver (rendezvous).

choices.label: The link has an infinite length.
choices.text: The link has an infinite length.

choices.label: The sender can queue up to 'n' messages.
choices.text: The sender can queue up to 'n' messages.

questions.explanation: Zero capacity means no messages are queued on a link; thus, the sender must wait for the receiver to receive the message.

questions.text: Which of the following defines a 'Socket' as used in client-server communication?

choices.label: A C-structure in the Linux kernel.
choices.text: A C-structure in the Linux kernel.

choices.label: An endpoint for communication, defined by an IP address concatenated with a port number.
choices.text: An endpoint for communication, defined by an IP address concatenated with a port number.

choices.label: A proxy for a remote procedure.
choices.text: A proxy for a remote procedure.

choices.label: A unidirectional pipe between a parent and child.
choices.text: A unidirectional pipe between a parent and child.

questions.explanation: A socket is an endpoint for communication, specifically the concatenation of an IP address and a port number.

questions.text: In Remote Procedure Calls (RPC), what is the role of a 'Stub'?

choices.label: To allocate shared memory segments.
choices.text: To allocate shared memory segments.

choices.label: To act as a client-side proxy that locates the server and marshalls parameters.
choices.text: To act as a client-side proxy that locates the server and marshalls parameters.

choices.label: To act as the main execution loop of the operating system.
choices.text: To act as the main execution loop of the operating system.

choices.label: To prevent cascading termination.
choices.text: To prevent cascading termination.

questions.explanation: Stubs are client-side proxies for the actual procedure on the server. The client-side stub locates the server and marshalls the parameters.

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding 'Ordinary Pipes'?

choices.label: They are bidirectional and allow full-duplex communication.
choices.text: They are bidirectional and allow full-duplex communication.

choices.label: They can be accessed by any process on the network.
choices.text: They can be accessed by any process on the network.

choices.label: They are typically unidirectional and require a parent-child relationship.
choices.text: They are typically unidirectional and require a parent-child relationship.

choices.label: They do not require the process to be in the 'Running' state.
choices.text: They do not require the process to be in the 'Running' state.

questions.explanation: Ordinary pipes are unidirectional and require a relationship (like parent-child) between the communicating processes.

questions.text: How do 'Named Pipes' differ from 'Ordinary Pipes'? Select all that apply:

choices.label: Named Pipes are bidirectional.
choices.text: Named Pipes are bidirectional.

choices.label: No parent-child relationship is necessary for Named Pipes.
choices.text: No parent-child relationship is necessary for Named Pipes.

choices.label: Named Pipes are only available on UNIX, not Windows.
choices.text: Named Pipes are only available on UNIX, not Windows.

choices.label: Several processes can use a Named Pipe for communication.
choices.text: Several processes can use a Named Pipe for communication.

questions.explanation: Named Pipes are bidirectional, do not require a parent-child relationship, and can be used by several processes. They are available on both UNIX and Windows.
